answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe what you were doing when the elbow pain started or if there was any injury?
answers.intake_a2: I was playing tennis when the pain started
answers.intake_q3: Can you describe the location of the pain on your elbow and if it radiates anywhere?
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any weakness or difficulty gripping objects with that hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any numbness, tingling, or changes in sensation in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and if so, what was the effect?
answers.intake_a7: I iced it the first day and it helped a little
answers.intake_q8: Any pain with wrist or finger movements?
answers.intake_q9: Is the pain worse when you try to extend your wrist or straighten your fingers?
answers.intake_q10: Does the pain worsen with gripping or lifting activities?
answers.intake_a10: the is pain with lifting objects

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and does not radiate. There is no swelling, redness, or warmth noted around the elbow. She denies weakness, numbness, tingling, or sensory changes in the arm or hand. The pain worsens with lifting objects but is not aggravated by wrist or finger movements, including extension or straightening of the fingers. Initial icing provided some mild relief. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vitals available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by lifting and activity involving the forearm extensor muscles. There is no evidence of neurological involvement or inflammatory signs.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; patient currently has no known renal or liver disease. Avoid NSAIDs in patients with allergy or intolerance. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow, or if there is no improvement after 4-6 weeks of conservative management. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist or worsen despite treatment.

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…
